items.title: Track matrix shapes explicitly while deriving and coding
items.avoid: Relying on intuition about shapes or using trial-and-error to make code run without ever writing down dimensions.
items.prefer: Annotate each symbol in derivations and code with its shape (for example, W: features x outputs) and verify inner dimensions match before multiplying.
items.why: Shape annotations make it easier to reason about compatibility, interpret transposes correctly and catch bugs early, especially in high-dimensional ML models.
items.example:
```python
# X: batch x din, W: din x dout
Y = X @ W  # Y: batch x dout
```

items.title: Use associativity to minimise computation cost
items.avoid: Multiplying matrices in an arbitrary order, such as always computing products from left to right, without considering the relative dimensions.
items.prefer: Regroup chains of matrix products to reduce the total number of scalar multiplications, for example computing A(BC) instead of (AB)C when this reduces intermediate dimensions.
items.why: For large matrices, the difference in computational cost between two valid parenthesisations can be orders of magnitude, which directly impacts training and inference latency.
items.example: If A is 1000 x 10, B is 10 x 100 and C is 100 x 5, then (AB)C does about 1.5e6 multiplications, while A(BC) does about 5.5e4, even though both yield the same result.

items.title: Prefer optimised linear algebra libraries over hand-written loops
items.avoid: Implementing matrix multiplication with pure Python loops in performance-critical code such as training or inference paths.
items.prefer: Use well-optimised primitives like NumPy, PyTorch or BLAS-backed libraries that implement matrix multiplication in compiled code and can leverage vectorised instructions and accelerators.
items.why: Hand-written nested loops in Python are orders of magnitude slower than library calls that use low-level optimisations and can target GPUs or specialised accelerators.
items.example:
```python
# Slow
C = [[sum(a*b for a, b in zip(row, col)) for col in zip(*B)] for row in A]

# Fast
C = A_array @ B_array
```
